results.possibleIdentity: Daniel Hampel was an electronics engineer active from the 1950s through the 1980s, primarily known for his work at the Radio Corporation of America (RCA). He received his BSEE in 1953 and MSEE in 1958 from the Newark College of Engineering. His early career included positions at Bell Telephone Laboratories (1953–1957), where he worked on electronic switching systems, and ITT Laboratories, where he was a Senior Engineer in the Countermeasures Laboratory. By 1960, he joined RCA, eventually working within the Advanced Communications Laboratory and the Government Communications Systems Division in Somerville and Camden, New Jersey. His technical expertise was centered on digital electronics, specifically threshold logic (weighted logic gates), CMOS technology, and VLSI (Very Large Scale Integration) design. He authored numerous technical papers and held several patents, including designs for storage circuits and MOS threshold logic devices. In the late 1960s and 1970s, he resided in Westfield, New Jersey. His professional background in high-level electronics at RCA and Bell Labs during the 1960s aligns with the technical staff who participated in the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) collaborations, which sought to pair artists with engineers from major New Jersey technical institutions.

segment.text: He received his BSEE in 1953 and MSEE in 1958 from the Newark College of Engineering.

His early career included positions at Bell Telephone Laboratories (1953–1957), where he worked on electronic switching systems, and ITT Laboratories, where he was a Senior Engineer in the Countermeasures Laboratory.
In the late 1960s and 1970s, he resided in Westfield, New Jersey.
